name: Zhuangzi
shortDescription: Zhuangzi was a highly influential Chinese philosopher who lived during the Warring States period (476–221 BCE) and is credited with writing (at least partially) the foundational text of philosophical Taoism, also known as the Zhuangzi.
description: Zhuangzi (莊子), also known as Master Zhuang, was a pivotal figure in the development of Taoism. Living during a time of great social and political upheaval in ancient China, Zhuangzi advocated for a philosophy of detachment, spontaneity, and living in harmony with the Tao (the Way). His teachings, primarily found in the book bearing his name, the Zhuangzi, emphasize the limitations of human knowledge and the importance of embracing the natural flow of existence. He criticized the rigid social structures and moralistic doctrines of Confucianism, promoting instead a life of freedom and self-cultivation. Zhuangzi's philosophy is characterized by its use of parables, anecdotes, and imaginative narratives to convey complex ideas. Key concepts include 'wu wei' (non-action or effortless action), 'ziran' (naturalness or spontaneity), and the transcendence of dualistic thinking. He also explored the nature of perception and the relativity of values, challenging conventional notions of good and evil, beauty and ugliness. The Zhuangzi text is divided into 'inner chapters' believed to be written by Zhuangzi himself and 'outer' and 'miscellaneous chapters' attributed to his followers and later writers. His influence extends beyond Taoism, impacting Chinese art, literature, and culture, as well as influencing Western thought on topics of existentialism and environmentalism. The Zhuangzi provides a unique perspective on how to live a meaningful life amidst chaos and uncertainty, advocating for a return to the simplicity and wisdom of nature.

sources.title: Wandering on the Way: Early Taoist Tales and Parables of Chuang Tzu
sources.author: Victor H. Mair (Translator)
